verdicts.reasoning: Course directly addresses the Nutrition core idea of OSPI K-12 Health standards: macronutrients, micronutrients, food's role in growth/development, nutrition-related disease prevention, and developing a personal healthy dietary plan. The applied component (students create dietary plans for themselves and community members) aligns with personal nutritional planning, health-enhancing behaviors, and advocacy strands. While it includes anatomy/physiology of the GI tract (leaning college-level), the central focus on personal nutrition planning and disease prevention satisfies the Health Nutrition strand. Science (Non-Lab) appropriately captures the biological/physiological content.

College-level nutrition science course focused on human metabolism, biochemistry of nutrients, and digestive physiology — prerequisite is AP&100/103 or BIOL&160, and it's designed for health occupations/nursing students. Content emphasizes scientific process of digestion, macro/micronutrient functions, and disease processes at a science level, not K-12 personal health literacy (nutritional planning, label literacy, personal eating habits skill-building). Matches the "Nutrition science as a college-level biochemistry/dietetics course" exclusion. Retain Science (Non-Lab).

College-level nutrition science course focused on biochemistry/physiology of macronutrients and metabolism — explicitly labeled "Natural Science" with prereqs in biology/chemistry. This aligns with college science (biochemistry/dietetics track), NOT K-12 personal nutrition planning, food groups, label literacy, or caloric intake skill-building required by OSPI Health standards. The description emphasizes physiological requirements and metabolism rather than personal eating behaviors or health-enhancing decision-making.

Course directly addresses the K-12 Health Nutrition strand: food groups/nutrients, label literacy, caloric/energy balance, nutritional planning across the lifecycle, disease prevention from excesses/deficiencies, and analyzing influences (socioeconomic, cultural, media) on personal eating habits. Also covers body image and consumer literacy. This is personal nutrition literacy, not college-level biochemistry/dietetics professional training, so it aligns with OSPI Health standards. Retaining Science (Lab) as currently classified.

Course focuses on professional care navigation, coordination, and advocacy roles within health/human services systems — this is workforce/professional-track healthcare training (community health worker / care coordinator pathway), not K-12 personal health literacy. It does not address the OSPI Health strands (personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, safety, nutrition) through individual skill-building. Aligns with CTE Health Sciences pathway rather than the Health graduation strand.

Course focuses on healthcare delivery systems, community health practice, and cross-cultural healthcare provision for clinicians/educators/researchers serving global populations. This is professional-track public health/healthcare administration content, not K-12 personal health literacy. Does not address OSPI's 6 Core Ideas (wellness, safety, nutrition, sexual health, SEL, substance use) at a personal/family/community skill-building level.

Course is population/community health focused — examines social, political, economic, environmental, biomedical aspects of community health, health disparities/inequities, and professional concepts like secondary trauma, compassion fatigue, and burnout. This is college-level public health policy analysis, not K-12 personal health literacy. Does not address required HS Health strands (personal wellness, sexual health per Healthy Youth Act, substance use prevention, CPR/AED, personal nutrition, suicide prevention skills).

Course focuses on healthcare systems navigation, health insurance, case management, confidentiality protocols, and community/population health advocacy — these are professional/workforce-prep topics (community health worker training, healthcare delivery systems) explicitly listed as NOT aligned with K-12 Health standards. While it mentions mental health first aid and signs of chronic illness, the dominant frame is systems-level public health advocacy, not personal health literacy or behavior skills. Belongs in CTE Health Sciences pathway or as Social Studies elective covering health policy/community systems.

Course focuses on data collection/management, information exchange, and strategic communication design for community health audiences — this is healthcare informatics and professional health communications/program planning, not K-12 personal health literacy. Description contains no OSPI Health strands (wellness, safety, nutrition, sexual health, SEL/mental health, substance use). Aligns with professional/workforce-prep coursework (health informatics, health promotion program planning) explicitly excluded from the OSPI Health graduation strand.

Course is healthcare policy/law/ethics at population level — examining policy tradeoffs between public welfare and individual rights. Does not address K-12 personal health strands (personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, safety skills, personal nutrition). Aligns with public health policy analysis and healthcare law, explicitly listed as NOT K-12 Health.
Population-level epidemiology/public health methods, not K-12 personal health literacy strands.

Course focuses on population health (a public/systems-level discipline), healthcare quality and safety, the "four pillars of population health," and theoretical frameworks for understanding health behaviors at a population/community level. This is professional/college-level public health coursework — explicitly excluded from K-12 Health standards which target personal health literacy and skill-building (the 6 Core Ideas: Wellness, Safety, Nutrition, Sexual Health, SEL, Substance Use). The course does not address WA's required HS Health content (CPR/AED, sexual health/Healthy Youth Act, suicide prevention, substance use prevention, personal nutrition planning). Patient education and healthcare quality/safety reference clinical/healthcare delivery contexts, not personal health behaviors.

College-level professional course on program planning/evaluation for healthcare and health promotion delivery — focuses on systems-level program development, implementation frameworks, evidence-based models, resource logistics, and data collection methodology. This is health education program planning and evaluation (PHE-major workforce coursework), explicitly listed as NOT aligned with K-12 OSPI Health standards. No personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental health, safety, or personal nutrition content. Best fits CTE Health Sciences pathway.

Course teaches research methodology (quantitative/qualitative designs, hypotheses, variables, validity/reliability, surveys, content analysis, focus groups, interviewing) applied to healthcare — explicitly a professional/college research methods course. None of the OSPI K-12 Health strands (personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, safety, nutrition) are addressed. Best fits Science (Non-Lab) as a research methods/applied science course; alternatively could fit Elective.

Course covers professional/organizational emergency management (threat assessment, EOC staffing, interagency coordination, incident command, response planning) — a homeland security/public safety workforce topic, not K-12 personal health literacy. OSPI's Safety strand under Health addresses personal Injury Prevention, First Aid, Violence Prevention, and CPR/AED (RCW 28A.230.179), not systems-level disaster response operations. Aligns with CTE Public Safety/Homeland Security pathway or general Elective credit.

Course focuses on comparative healthcare delivery systems, community health practice, and cultural competency for clinicians/educators/researchers serving global populations. This is professional/systems-level public health and healthcare delivery analysis, not K-12 personal health literacy. It addresses none of the OSPI Core Ideas (Wellness, Safety, Nutrition, Sexual Health, SEL/Mental Health, Substance Use) at the personal-skill-building level. Aligns with college-level global/public health policy, which the context explicitly excludes from Health credit. Best fit is Social Studies Elective due to cross-cultural/sociological focus, or could be Elective.

Course is part of the Community Health Professions (CHP) program at Tacoma Community College, which is a college-level professional/workforce track in public/community health — not K-12 personal health literacy. The 399 designation indicates an upper-level independent study within that professional pathway. Community health profession coursework focuses on population-level health promotion, program planning, and workforce-prep rather than the OSPI 6 Core Ideas (personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, etc.). The vague independent-study description does not demonstrate coverage of the required K-12 Health strands (CPR/AED, sexual health per Healthy Youth Act, suicide prevention, substance use, nutrition planning, etc.). Without a defined syllabus tied to OSPI standards, this cannot satisfy HS Health credit. Elective is the safest replacement given the unspecified independent-study content.

Course covers environmental policy, regulation, occupational health, waste disposal, and toxic exposure at a systems/policy level — not K-12 personal health literacy strands (wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, nutrition planning, CPR/first aid). Aligns with environmental science content rather than OSPI Health standards.

Course teaches Public Health and Social-Ecological Models of trauma at a population/systems level — analyzing community and societal influences that create trauma. This is public health framework analysis, not K-12 personal health literacy (no personal wellness skills, no sexual health, no substance use prevention skills, no nutrition, no safety/first aid, no personal mental health coping skills). Aligns with social studies / sociology analysis of community-level factors rather than OSPI Health standards' skill-based personal/family health focus.

This is a college-level family studies/sociology course examining family as a social system, family processes, and social/cultural contexts of family development. While it mentions "five areas of health" and "health and wellness," the focus is on sociological/systems analysis of families rather than personal health literacy skills required by OSPI K-12 Health standards. It does not address the required strands (sexual health/HIV per RCW, substance use, CPR/AED, nutrition planning, mental health skill-building). The content aligns with social sciences/sociology of the family, fitting Social Studies - Elective.

Course focuses on epidemiology, healthcare worker roles, cost/benefit analysis of interventions, emergency preparedness, and government/non-government agency responsibilities — all population-level public health policy and systems content, not personal K-12 health literacy strands (wellness, nutrition, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, safety skills like CPR/AED).

Course examines culture-health relationships at the community/population level to inform "community health work" and reduce "health disparities" — this is professional/workforce-oriented community health worker training and medical sociology/health disparities content, not K-12 personal health literacy. It does not address required HS Health strands (personal wellness, nutrition, sexual health, mental health, substance use, safety/CPR) or any RCW-mandated content. Aligns with the explicit non-Health exclusions: "Community health worker / health promotion professional training" and "Medical sociology / health disparities research methodology." Best fit is Social Studies - Elective (cultural/sociological lens) or general Elective.

Capstone course for a Bachelor of Applied Science (BAS) Community Health Professional concentration. This is workforce-prep coursework for a community health professional career, explicitly listed as misaligned with K-12 Health standards ("Community health worker / health promotion professional training"). It is a senior-level college capstone, not personal health literacy aligned with the 6 OSPI Core Ideas. Best fit is CTE as a professional/workforce-track health sciences capstone.

Professional EMS leadership/ethics course — workforce-prep healthcare administration, not K-12 personal health literacy strands (wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental/emotional health, safety, nutrition).

Emergency Management is a professional/workforce field focused on leadership and management roles for disaster response — not K-12 personal health literacy. Course covers vocabulary, core components, historical events, and leadership/management roles of the emergency management field, which is systems-level/professional rather than personal wellness, safety skills (first aid/CPR), sexual health, substance use, mental health, or nutrition. Aligns with CTE (public safety/emergency services pathway) or Social Studies elective (policy/government response to disasters), not the OSPI Health strand.

Course is a college-level public health / health promotion overview focused on population-level issues — major causes of premature mortality/morbidity, environmental contributions to illness, economic, public policy, and ethical issues in health promotion. This is health policy and public health systems content, explicitly listed as NOT aligned with K-12 Health (which focuses on personal health literacy and skill-building). No coverage of required RCW topics (CPR/AED, sexual health, suicide prevention, substance use prevention at personal level). The "CHPM" prefix (Community Health Promotion) and 400-level number confirm professional/upper-division public health coursework.

Community Paramedicine is professional/clinical healthcare workforce training for advanced paramedics — covers health assessments, chronic disease monitoring, medication management, immunizations, lab specimen collection, and minor medical procedures performed on patients. This is clinical practitioner training, not K-12 personal health literacy. It does not address OSPI's 6 Core Ideas (personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, social-emotional health, nutrition for personal eating, personal safety/CPR) as skill-building for one's own health. Belongs in the CTE Health Sciences pathway as professional pre-licensure coursework.

Clinical internship for Community Paramedicine is professional EMS workforce training involving supervised patient care delivery in community healthcare settings. This is healthcare professional practice, not K-12 personal health literacy. Aligns with CTE Health Sciences pathway, not OSPI Health standards which focus on personal wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental health, and safety skill-building.
Professional paramedic critical care training (FP-C/CCP-C exam prep) — clinical workforce/CTE Health Sciences, not K-12 personal health literacy strands.
Critical Care Transport Internship is a clinical ICU rotation for professional paramedic/healthcare workforce training, not K-12 personal health literacy. Aligns with CTE Health Sciences pathway.

EMS Capstone is a professional paramedic-track clinical course covering critical care, community paramedic, or EMS leadership specialties. This is workforce/CTE healthcare pre-professional training, not K-12 personal health literacy aligned with OSPI's 6 Core Ideas. Belongs in CTE Health Sciences pathway.

Professional clinical training in treating tobacco dependence (neuropharmacology, pharmacologic management, counseling sessions, motivational interviewing, co-morbid conditions). This is practitioner/workforce-prep coursework, not K-12 personal substance use prevention/health literacy.

Course is professional healthcare leadership/supervision training, not K-12 personal health literacy. Addresses healthcare administration and management skills — explicitly excluded from OSPI Health standards. Aligns with CTE healthcare pathway.

Course trains healthcare professionals to deliver education to peers/students/clients - workforce-prep teaching methods for practitioners, not K-12 personal health literacy (wellness, sexual health, substance use, mental health, safety, nutrition).
Advanced clinical respiratory therapy training for credentialed practitioners (adult/neonatal/pediatric critical care); professional-track healthcare, not K-12 personal health literacy.

Advanced Respiratory Care Capstone is a clinical pre-professional course in advanced practice specialties (critical care, neonatal, pediatrics, management, or education). This is workforce/clinical training, not K-12 personal health literacy. Aligns with CTE Health Sciences pathway, not the OSPI Health graduation strand.

Course is college-level nutrition science focused on chemistry of nutrients and biology of the GI tract — aligns with college biochemistry/dietetics framing explicitly flagged as NOT K-12 Health. Lacks the personal nutritional planning, label literacy, and skill-based health behaviors required by OSPI Nutrition strand. Retain Science (Non-Lab).
